questions.question: Define Student's t-distribution and state two situations where it is used instead of the normal distribution.
keyPoints: Student's t-distribution is a probability distribution used for small sample estimation
It is used when sample size n < 30 and population standard deviation is unknown
Also used when population is normally distributed but with limited data

questions.question: State three properties of the Student's t-distribution.
keyPoints: It is symmetric about zero (bell-shaped)
It has heavier tails than the normal distribution
Mean = 0 for df > 1, variance = df/(df-2) for df > 2
It approaches standard normal as df → ∞

questions.question: Explain the concept of degrees of freedom in the context of t-distribution.
keyPoints: Degrees of freedom = n - 1 for one-sample t-test
Represents number of independent values that can vary
Increasing df makes t-distribution approach normal distribution
With n observations estimating mean, only n-1 can vary freely

questions.question: Write the formula for calculating the t-statistic in a one-sample t-test and explain each component.
keyPoints: x̄ = sample mean, μ = population mean (hypothesized)
s = sample standard deviation
s/√n = standard error of the mean
This measures how many standard errors the sample mean is from the population mean

questions.question: How does the t-distribution differ from the standard normal distribution?
keyPoints: T-distribution has heavier tails than normal distribution
T-distribution is flatter and more spread out
Difference is most pronounced for small df (small samples)
As df increases, t-distribution converges to normal distribution

questions.question: What is a paired t-test? When is it appropriate to use it?
keyPoints: Paired t-test compares means from same subjects at different times or under different conditions
Used for before-after measurements on same individuals
Used for matched pairs of subjects
Test statistic: t = d̄ / (s_d/√n) where d̄ is mean of differences

questions.question: How do you construct a 95% confidence interval for population mean using t-distribution?
keyPoints: Formula: x̄ ± t_α/2 × (s/√n)
Find t_α/2 from t-table with df = n-1
For 95% CI, α = 0.05, so use t_0.025
This interval will contain true population mean in 95% of samples

questions.question: Derive the formula for the two-sample independent t-test assuming equal variances. Explain the concept of pooled variance.
keyPoints: State formula: t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂) / s_p × √(1/n₁ + 1/n₂)
Pooled variance combines both sample variances: s_p² = ((n₁-1)s₁² + (n₂-1)s₂²) / (n₁ + n₂ - 2)
Degrees of freedom = n₁ + n₂ - 2
Explain how pooled variance gives weighted average of both sample variances
Assumption: Both populations have equal variances (homoscedasticity)

questions.question: Explain the hypothesis testing procedure using Student's t-distribution. Include steps for both one-tailed and two-tailed tests.
keyPoints: State null and alternative hypotheses clearly
Choose significance level (α)
Calculate t-statistic from sample data
Find critical t-value from t-table using α and df
Compare calculated t with critical t
Decision: Reject H₀ if |t(calc)| > |t(crit)| for two-tailed
For one-tailed, reject if t(calc) > t(crit) (or < for lower tail)
State conclusion in context of the problem

questions.question: Compare and contrast Student's t-distribution with the standard normal distribution. Also explain the conditions under which each should be used.
keyPoints: Both are symmetric, bell-shaped distributions centered at zero
T-distribution has heavier tails - accounts for extra uncertainty in small samples
Normal: used when population σ is known or n ≥ 30
T-distribution: used when population σ is unknown and n < 30
As n increases (df increases), t approaches normal
Both assume underlying population is normally distributed

questions.question: A researcher wants to test whether a new teaching method improves student performance. A sample of 15 students showed mean score = 78 with standard deviation = 8. The national average is 72. Perform a t-test at α = 0.05 to determine if the new method is effective.
keyPoints: H₀: μ = 72 (no improvement), H₁: μ > 72 (improvement)
This is a one-tailed (right-tailed) test
Degrees of freedom = 15 - 1 = 14
Critical t for α = 0.05, df = 14 (one-tailed) = 2.145
Since 2.90 > 2.145, reject null hypothesis
Conclusion: Significant evidence that new method improves scores
